perturbation.original_text: Term Section 1.01. Term. The term of this Agreement shall be effective as of the date first stated above and shall continue for a term of three (3) years, unless terminated earlier in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement (the "Term"); provided, however, that PrimeCall may elect to terminate this Agreement, upon thirty (30) days' written notice, at any time from and after the time that collectively RSL Communications, Ltd. and/or its Affiliates holds less than fifty percent (50%) of the voting control of DeltaThree's outstanding shares.
perturbation.changed_text: Term Section 1.01. Term. The term of this Agreement shall be effective as of the date first stated above and shall continue indefinitely, with no option for termination, unless both parties mutually agree to terminate (the "Term"); provided, however, that PrimeCall may elect to terminate this Agreement, upon thirty (30) days' written notice, at any time from and after the time that collectively RSL Communications, Ltd. and/or its Affiliates holds less than fifty percent (50%) of the voting control of DeltaThree's outstanding shares.
perturbation.explanation: The contract originally provides a defined term of three years with specific termination clauses. Changing the term to 'indefinitely, with no option for termination' creates a contradiction, especially given the existing clause allowing PrimeCall to terminate under certain conditions. This also conflicts with basic contract law principles allowing for termination under specific circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-903 - Automatic renewal provision of contract for service, maintenance or repair unenforceable by contractor unless notice thereof given to recipient of services
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-903 addresses automatic renewal clauses, which are related to the duration and termination of contracts. By making the term indefinite with no option for termination, the contract essentially becomes perpetually binding, hindering the ability to terminate. This clause can be seen as a restriction on the recipient to end the services, and no notice may have been given. The altered clause is problematic under New York law because it could be interpreted as an unconscionable term, particularly if one party is significantly disadvantaged by the lack of termination options.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 9 § 5-903. Automatic renewal provision of contract for service, maintenance or repair unenforceable by contractor unless notice thereof given to recipient of services. 1. As used in this section, "person" means an individual, firm, company, partnership or corporation. 2. No provision of a contract for service, maintenance or repair to or for any real or personal property which states that the term of the contract shall be deemed renewed for a specified additional period unless the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair gives notice to the person furnishing such contract service, maintenance or repair of his intention to terminate the contract at the expiration of such term, shall be enforceable against the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair, unless the person furnishing the service, maintenance or repair, at least fifteen days and not more than thirty days previous to the time specified for serving such notice upon him, shall give to the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair written notice, served personally or by certified mail, calling the attention of that person to the existence of such provision in the contract. 3. Nothing herein contained shall be construed to apply to a contract in which the automatic renewal period specified is one month or less.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction by changing the term to 'indefinitely, with no option for termination', which conflicts with the original agreement's termination clauses and general contract law principles. NY GOL § 5-903, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses automatic renewal provisions, which relate to the duration and termination of contracts. The modified clause restricts the ability to terminate, which is problematic under New York law. The altered term effectively makes the contract perpetually binding, which can be seen as an unconscionable term, especially if one party is disadvantaged by the lack of termination options.

perturbation.original_text: Section 8.01 [Confidentiality]. Confidentiality. PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree that for the longest period permitted by law each shall hold in strictest confidence and, without the prior written approval of the other party hereto, not to use for their own benefit or the benefit of any party other than the other party hereto, or disclose to any person, firm or corporation other than such party (other than as required by law) any confidential proprietary information concerning the business and affairs of the other party hereto; provided, however, that the foregoing limitations and restrictions shall not apply to information that (i) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by the directors, officers, shareholders, partners, affiliates, employees, agents or advisors of PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, or (ii) is or becomes available to PrimeCall or DeltaThree on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the other party hereto or any of its advisors, agents or affiliates, provided that such source is not known by PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the other party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 8.01 [Confidentiality]. Confidentiality. PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree that for the longest period permitted by law each shall hold in strictest confidence and, without the prior written approval of the other party hereto, not to use for their own benefit or the benefit of any party other than the other party hereto, or disclose to any person, firm or corporation other than such party (other than as required by law) any confidential proprietary information concerning the business and affairs of the other party hereto; this obligation extends indefinitely, without exception.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for exceptions to confidentiality if information becomes publicly available or is received from another source without confidentiality obligations. The modified version removes these exceptions and makes the confidentiality obligation absolute and indefinite. This creates a legal contradiction because it ignores the reality that some information inevitably becomes public knowledge.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret and requires that the information derive economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By imposing an absolute and indefinite confidentiality obligation, the modified contract attempts to protect information even if it becomes generally known or readily ascertainable, which contradicts the fundamental requirements for trade secret protection under the UTSA. A trade secret ceases to exist when it becomes public knowledge, and the modified clause ignores this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes exceptions to confidentiality, creating an absolute and indefinite obligation. The law explanation states that the UTSA requires trade secrets to derive economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By attempting to protect information even if it becomes public, the modified contract contradicts this fundamental requirement. Although the scraped snippets are either missing or irrelevant, the core principle remains that information cannot be protected as a trade secret if it is already public. Thus, imposing indefinite confidentiality creates a contradiction as it ignores the public domain, as the Perturbation Explanation suggests.

perturbation.original_text: Section 9.04 [Independent Contractors]. Independent Contractors. At all times the parties hereto shall be considered independent contractors and this Agreement shall not create any agency, partnership or employment relationship between the parties. Except as specifically set forth herein, neither party shall have the right to act for or on behalf of or in the name of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 9.04 [Independent Contractors]. Independent Contractors. At all times the parties hereto shall be considered independent contractors; however, DeltaThree shall have the sole discretion to dictate the specific methods and means by which PrimeCall fulfills its obligations under this agreement. This Agreement shall not create any agency, partnership or employment relationship between the parties. Except as specifically set forth herein, neither party shall have the right to act for or on behalf of or in the name of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause explicitly states that the parties are independent contractors. The added clause, 'DeltaThree shall have the sole discretion to dictate the specific methods and means by which PrimeCall fulfills its obligations under this agreement,' introduces a level of control that is inconsistent with independent contractor status. This level of control suggests an employer-employee relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 516.2 pertains to record keeping requirements under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The FLSA distinguishes between employees and independent contractors, and this distinction hinges on the level of control exerted by the employer. The modified clause, by granting DeltaThree control over PrimeCall's methods, suggests an employment relationship, potentially making PrimeCall employees subject to FLSA protections, including overtime and minimum wage requirements. This directly contradicts the stated intention of the agreement to treat PrimeCall as an independent contractor, and could lead to misclassification under the FLSA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fact Sheet 13: Employment Relationship Under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) | U.S. Department of Labor Skip to main content Print Fact Sheet WAGE AND HOUR DIVISION UNITED STATES DEPARTMENT OF LABOR This Fact Sheet is consistent with the 2024 Rule entitled Employee or Independent Contractor Classification Under the Fair Labor Standards Act, 89 Fed. Reg. 1638 – the legality of which is the subject of current litigation – that remains in effect for purposes of private litigation. Please see Field Assistance Bulletin 2025-1 (May 1, 2025) regarding the Wage and Hour Division’s current enforcement position. Fact Sheet 13: Employee or Independent Contractor Classification Under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) Revised March 2024 Is a Worker an Employee or an Independent Contractor? The Department has issued regulations addressing how to analyze whether a worker is an employee or an independent contractor under the FLSA (29 CFR part 795, effective March 11, 2024 ). Employees receive the protections of the FLSA. Independent contractors are in business for themselves and therefore are not covered by the FLSA. For a worker to be protected by the minimum wage and overtime pay requirements of the FLSA, the worker must be an “employee” of the employer, meaning that there is an employment relationship between the worker and employer. Independent contractors do not have these protections. Whether a worker is an employee or an independent contractor under the FLSA is determined by looking at the economic realities of the worker’s relationship with the employer. If the economic realities show that the worker is economically dependent on the employer for work, then the worker is an employee. If the economic realities show that the worker is in business for themself, then the worker is an independent contractor. The economic realities of the entire working relationship are looked at to decide whether a worker is an employee or an independent contractor. Employment under the FLSA is not determined by technical concepts or common law standards of control; it is broader than the common law standard often applied to determine employment status under other Federal laws. What Is the Economic Reality Test? The economic reality test uses multiple factors to see if an employment relationship exists under the FLSA (29 CFR 795.110). The goal of the test is to decide if the worker is economically dependent on the employer for work or is instead in business for themself. All factors should be considered. No single factor determines a worker’s status, and no one factor or combination of factors are more important than the other factors. Instead, the totality of the circumstances of the working relationship should be considered. The following factors, discussed more below, should guide the assessment of whether a worker is an employee under the FLSA or an independent contractor in business for themself: Opportu
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause that gives DeltaThree control over the means by which PrimeCall fulfills its obligations, contradicting the stated intention of the agreement that the parties are independent contractors. This contradicts the law, which defines independent contractors as being in business for themselves, and not economically dependent on the employer (Snippet 2). Granting DeltaThree such control suggests an employer-employee relationship, because the economic realities may show that the worker is economically dependent on the employer for work, then the worker is an employee (Snippet 2).
